ExtraaLearn Project¶

Context¶

The EdTech industry has been surging in the past decade immensely, and according to a forecast, the Online Education market would be worth $286.62bn by 2023 with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 10.26% from 2018 to 2023. The modern era of online education has enforced a lot in its growth and expansion beyond any limit. Due to having many dominant features like ease of information sharing, personalized learning experience, transparency of assessment, etc, it is now preferable to traditional education.

In the present scenario due to the Covid-19, the online education sector has witnessed rapid growth and is attracting a lot of new customers. Due to this rapid growth, many new companies have emerged in this industry. With the availability and ease of use of digital marketing resources, companies can reach out to a wider audience with their offerings. The customers who show interest in these offerings are termed as leads. There are various sources of obtaining leads for Edtech companies, like

  • The customer interacts with the marketing front on social media or other online platforms.
  • The customer browses the website/app and downloads the brochure
  • The customer connects through emails for more information.

The company then nurtures these leads and tries to convert them to paid customers. For this, the representative from the organization connects with the lead on call or through email to share further details.

Objective¶

ExtraaLearn is an initial stage startup that offers programs on cutting-edge technologies to students and professionals to help them upskill/reskill. With a large number of leads being generated on a regular basis, one of the issues faced by ExtraaLearn is to identify which of the leads are more likely to convert so that they can allocate resources accordingly. You, as a data scientist at ExtraaLearn, have been provided the leads data to:

  • Analyze and build an ML model to help identify which leads are more likely to convert to paid customers,
  • Find the factors driving the lead conversion process
  • Create a profile of the leads which are likely to convert

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of leads and their interaction details with ExtraaLearn. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary

  • ID: ID of the lead

  • age: Age of the lead

  • current_occupation: Current occupation of the lead. Values include 'Professional','Unemployed',and 'Student'

  • first_interaction: How did the lead first interacted with ExtraaLearn. Values include 'Website', 'Mobile App'

  • profile_completed: What percentage of profile has been filled by the lead on the website/mobile app. Values include Low - (0-50%), Medium - (50-75%), High (75-100%)

  • website_visits: How many times has a lead visited the website

  • time_spent_on_website: Total time spent on the website

  • page_views_per_visit: Average number of pages on the website viewed during the visits.

  • last_activity: Last interaction between the lead and ExtraaLearn.

    • Email Activity: Seeking for details about program through email, Representative shared information with lead like brochure of program , etc
    • Phone Activity: Had a Phone Conversation with representative, Had conversation over SMS with representative, etc
    • Website Activity: Interacted on live chat with representative, Updated profile on website, etc

  • print_media_type1: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn in the Newspaper.

  • print_media_type2: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn in the Magazine.

  • digital_media: Flag indicating whether the lead had seen the ad of ExtraaLearn on the digital platforms.

  • educational_channels: Flag indicating whether the lead had heard about ExtraaLearn in the education channels like online forums, discussion threads, educational websites, etc.

  • referral: Flag indicating whether the lead had heard about ExtraaLearn through reference.

  • status: Flag indicating whether the lead was converted to a paid customer or not.

Importing necessary libraries and data¶

In [1]:
# Standard libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Data plotting libraries
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Importing the Machine Learning libraries
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn import tree
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Importing the other functions
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix,classification_report

# ignore warnings
import warnings;
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Questions

  1. Leads will have different expectations from the outcome of the course and the current occupation may play a key role in getting them to participate in the program. Find out how current occupation affects lead status.
  2. The company's first impression on the customer must have an impact. Do the first channels of interaction have an impact on the lead status?
  3. The company uses multiple modes to interact with prospects. Which way of interaction works best?
  4. The company gets leads from various channels such as print media, digital media, referrals, etc. Which of these channels have the highest lead conversion rate?
  5. People browsing the website or mobile application are generally required to create a profile by sharing their personal data before they can access additional information.Does having more details about a prospect increase the chances of conversion?

Data Preprocessing¶

  • Missing value treatment (if needed)
  • Feature engineering (if needed)
  • Outlier detection and treatment (if needed)
  • Preparing data for modeling
  • Any other preprocessing steps (if needed)

Observations:

  • All columns have the same amount of entries (4612), which is also the amount of rows of this dataset. So there is no missing data.
  • Only age, website visits, time spent on the website, page views per visit and the status are numerical. The other ones are objects and mostly categorical. However, many of those can be see as booleans as well (e.g. all the flags like digital_media or referral)

Observations:

We make (only for the bi-variate analysis) some dummy columns of the flags with a numerical value (similar to the status column), which we will drop later again.

In [12]:
df['print_media_type1_num'] = df['print_media_type1'].map({'Yes': 1, 'No': 0})
df['print_media_type2_num'] = df['print_media_type2'].map({'Yes': 1, 'No': 0})
df['digital_media_num'] = df['digital_media'].map({'Yes': 1, 'No': 0})
df['educational_channels_num'] = df['educational_channels'].map({'Yes': 1, 'No': 0})
df['referral_num'] = df['referral'].map({'Yes': 1, 'No': 0})

There are no double entries. We can drop this column.

df = df.drop(['ID'], axis = 1)

Univariate analysis numerical¶

Before we start the univariate analysis, we make some functions to plot boxplots and histograms. In contrast with the previous assessment, we will plot the boxplot and histogram with a shared horizontal axis, to make the analysis easier

In [15]:
def variable_name(input_string):
  """
  Maps input strings to their more readable counterpart.

  Args:
    input_string: The input string to be mapped.

  Returns:
    The more readable output string.
  """
  mapping = {'age': 'Age',
             'current_occupation': 'Current Occupation',
             'first_interaction': 'First Interaction',
             'profile_completed': 'Profile Completed',
             'website_visits': 'Website Visits',
             'time_spent_on_website': 'Time Spent on Website',
             'page_views_per_visit': 'Page Views per Visit',
             'last_activity': 'Last Activity',
             'print_media_type1': 'Newspaper',
             'print_media_type2': 'Magazine',
             'digital_media': 'Digital Plaform',
             'educational_channels': 'Educational Channel',
             'referral': 'Referral',
             'status': 'Status',
             }

  return mapping.get(input_string, input_string) # Default to input_string if not found
In [16]:
def hist_box_plot(df: pd.DataFrame, num_var: str, x_label:str=None, graph_title:str=None):
  """
  Creates a boxplot with below it a histogram on the same horizontal axis for a numerical variable in a Pandas DataFrame.

  Args:
      df (pd.DataFrame): The Pandas DataFrame containing the data.
      num_var (str): The name of the numerical variable to create the boxplot for.
      x_label (str): The x label in case we do not want to put the numerical variable name.

  Returns:
      None. This function creates a boxplot and histogram on the same horizontal axis and displays it using plt.show().
  """
  # Create the plots with a shared axis using seaborn
  figure, (ax_box, ax_his) = plt.subplots(2, sharex=True, figsize=(10, 7), gridspec_kw={'height_ratios': (0.10, 0.9)})
  # Adding a graph in each part
  sns.boxplot(data=df, x=num_var, ax=ax_box, showmeans=True)
  sns.histplot(data=df, x=num_var, kde=True, ax=ax_his)

  # Label the x-axis with the variable name
  if x_label is None:
    plt.xlabel(f'{variable_name(num_var)}')
  else:
    plt.title(x_label)

  # Add the overall title

  # Add a title to the boxplot
  if graph_title is None:
    plt.suptitle(f'Distribution of {variable_name(num_var)}')
  else:
    plt.suptitle(graph_title)

  # Display the boxplot
  plt.show()
Age¶
In [17]:
hist_box_plot(df, 'age')

The distribution is left-skewed.

  • We see that most people in the dataset are older than 50 and probably retired. This means that they probably have more time to take these courses.
  • There is a gap of people around 25-28, probably because this age group is very busy with other things, like taking care of little children.
  • There are no outliers
Website visits¶
In [18]:
hist_box_plot(df, 'website_visits')

The distribution is right-skewed.

  • Most people have 1 to 3 visits, with a peak at 2
  • Some people have more than 10 visits (up to 30). The boxplot shows them as outliers. They are however nothing to worry about for the further analysis as there are no consistently big gaps between them. There is not need to omit or correct them.
Time spent on website¶
In [19]:
hist_box_plot(df, 'time_spent_on_website')

The distribution of the time spent is quite interesting, with two peaks (modes):

  • A first group did not spent so much time, typically below 700 minutes (?) (the unit of time is not mentioned in the description, is it minutes or seconds?), with most people even less than 400 minutes.
  • A second group spent quite some more time, with a (much lower) peak around 2000 minutes.
Page views per visit¶
In [20]:
hist_box_plot(df, 'page_views_per_visit')

The distribution of the page views per visit is multi-modal:

  • The highest peak is around 2.3 page views per visit
  • There is a peak around 2.7 and 0 page views per visit
  • There a lower peaks around 5.5 and 8 page views per visit

This might all have something to do with the way the website is made or what the different persons are looking for, but we cannot answer on the real reason with the information we have available (it is also not relevant for this excercise).

There are quite some outliers (some even above 15), but there is no reason to believe this is faulty data. Let's however check the few data entries with more than 15 page views per visit.

In [21]:
df[df['page_views_per_visit']>15]
Out[21]:
age current_occupation first_interaction profile_completed website_visits time_spent_on_website page_views_per_visit last_activity print_media_type1 print_media_type2 digital_media educational_channels referral status print_media_type1_num print_media_type2_num digital_media_num educational_channels_num referral_num
4 23 Student Website High 4 600 16.914 Email Activity No No No No No 0 0 0 0 0 0
32 58 Professional Mobile App High 12 1151 18.434 Phone Activity No No No Yes No 0 0 0 0 1 0
268 57 Professional Mobile App Medium 3 1009 18.302 Phone Activity No No No No No 0 0 0 0 0 0

There does not look anything abnormal for these entries. Maybe these are just people who like to discover all the pages as much as possible.

Univariate analysis categorical¶

We define some functions to vizualise this easier. We will take the function written in the previous assessment as a base again.

In [22]:
def sorted_countplot(data, var_cat: str, label_rotation=None, annotation=True):
  """
  Creates a sorted count plot for a categorical variable in a Pandas DataFrame.

  Args:
      data (pd.DataFrame): The Pandas DataFrame containing the data.
      var_cat (str): The name of the categorical variable to plot.
      label_rotation (int, optional): The number of degrees to rotate the x-axis tick labels for better readability with many categories. Defaults to None (no rotation).
      annotation: Indicate if the percentage annotation needs to be shown or not.

  Returns:
      None. This function creates a count plot and displays it using plt.show().
  """
  # First set the correct order the data needs to be plotted
  # Get a list of the unique categorical variables
  lst = data[var_cat].unique()

  # We will first show the non-numeric data (starting with the highest occurance)
  non_numeric_elements = []
  # Then we will show the numeric (integer) elements (in numerical order)
  numeric_elements = []

  # We check for each variable if it can be recognized as an integer or not
  for item in lst:
    try:
      numeric_elements.append(int(item))
    except:
      non_numeric_elements.append(item)

  # Sort the numerical items (ascending)
  numeric_elements.sort()
  # Keep only the categorical data and order them
  data_categorical = data[data[var_cat].isin(non_numeric_elements)]
  non_numeric_elements = data_categorical[var_cat].value_counts().index.tolist()
  # This is the final order to plot
  order_categories = non_numeric_elements + numeric_elements

  # Plot the data in the order we made
  ax = sns.countplot(x =data[var_cat], data=data, order=order_categories, palette='Set2')
  # Add the titles and labels
  plt.title(f'Distribution of {variable_name(var_cat)}')
  plt.xlabel(f'{variable_name(var_cat)}')
  # Optionally rotate the x-axis tick labels
  if label_rotation is not None:
      plt.setp(ax.get_xticklabels(), rotation=label_rotation)

  # Define the annotation and add it to the plot
  if annotation == True:
    # Set ylim so the annotation is for sure in the graph
    # Set y-axis limit to 1.2 times the maximum count
    max_count = data[var_cat].value_counts().max()
    plt.ylim(0, 1.2 * max_count)


    # Total amount of data
    tot_count = data[var_cat].value_counts().sum()

    # Add a percentage annotation to the plot
    for p in ax.patches:
        x=p.get_bbox().get_points()[:,0]
        y=p.get_bbox().get_points()[1,1]
        if label_rotation is not None:
          ax.annotate('{:.1f}%'.format(100.*y/tot_count), (x.mean(), y+10),
                ha='center', va='bottom', rotation=label_rotation)
        else:
          ax.annotate('{:.1f}%'.format(100.*y/tot_count), (x.mean(), y+10),
                ha='center', va='bottom')

  plt.show()
Current occupation¶
In [23]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'current_occupation')

Observations:

  • More than half of the people are a professional
  • 30% is unemployed
  • The remaining 12% is student
First interaction¶
In [24]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'first_interaction')

The first interaction is almost 50-50, with a small advantage for the website (55%).

Profile completed¶
In [25]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'profile_completed')

Observations:

  • Half of the people filled in their profile almost completely (>75%)
  • The remaining other half filled it in partly
  • Only a very low percentage or 2.3% has a low filled profile

Note that for the analysis we could decide to convert this variable into a number (e.g. 1-3) indicating the level of profile completion. As we are however going to make Decision Tree and Random Forest models, this does not really change a lot on how they will model the data.

Last Activity¶
In [26]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'last_activity')

Observations:

  • About half of the last activities was over e-mail
  • The remaining two types (phone and website) contributed both to about a quarter of the total amount of interactions
Newspaper advertissement¶
In [27]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'print_media_type1')

Only 10.8% saw the newspaper add.

Magazine advertissement¶
In [28]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'print_media_type2')

Only 5.1% saw the magazine add.

Digital platform¶
In [29]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'digital_media')

Only 11.4% saw the add on the digital platforms.

Educational channels¶
In [30]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'educational_channels')

Only 15.3% saw the add on the educational channels. This is however the highest percentage of all adds that were placed.

This answers on of the questions mentioned before: educational channels are the most effective form of advertissement.

Referral¶
In [31]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'referral')

Only 2% of the people were contacted after a referral. This is a very small percentage. As we will see later in the bivariate analysis, these people have a high lead conversion rate.

Status¶

The most important parameter. We want to understand how all other features influence this one.

In [32]:
sorted_countplot(df, 'status')

The conversion to paid customers is not balanced. Only 30% of all people are buying customers already.

Bi-variate analysis numerical¶

To check the correlation between the numerical variables, the easiest way is to start with a correlation heatmap (similar to the previous assessment). Here our numerical versions of the flag columns will come in handy.

In [33]:
def corr_heatmap(df):
  # Check for correlation among numerical variables
  variables_list = ['age',	'website_visits',	'time_spent_on_website',	'page_views_per_visit',	'print_media_type1_num',	'print_media_type2_num',	'digital_media_num',	'educational_channels_num',	'referral_num',	'status']
  corr_matrix = df[variables_list].corr()

  # Plot the heatmap
  sns.heatmap(corr_matrix,
              cmap = 'coolwarm',
              fmt = ".2f",
              annot = True,
              xticklabels = corr_matrix.columns,
              yticklabels = corr_matrix.columns,
              linewidth=.5)

  # add title to heatmap
  plt.title('Correlation heatmap')

Show the heatmap:

In [34]:
corr_heatmap(df)

Observations:

  • We generally do not see a lot of strong correlations.
  • 'website_visits', 'time_spent_on_website' and 'page_views_per_visit' have logically (some weak) correlation, but it is not that strong.
  • We see a slight correlation between the 'time spent on the website' and the 'status'. This makes sense, as someone who is more interested might spend more time on the website.
  • We see even weaker correlations between 'age'/'referral' and the 'status'. This also makes sense, as a referral would mean the person already discussed it before with someone and is interested. The correlation with age might indicate that older people have more time and want to follow the courses.

Drop dummy numerical flag columns again¶

For the further analysis and building the model, we will use the object columns and not the dummy numerical ones

In [35]:
df = df.drop(['print_media_type1_num', 'print_media_type2_num', 'digital_media_num', 'educational_channels_num', 'referral_num'], axis = 1)

Bi-variate analysis categorical¶

For the bi-variate analysis in function of the status, the categorical predictors 'Current Occupation', 'First Interaction', 'Profile Completed' and 'Last Activity' still need to be looked at. In order to do this, the easiest method is to make stacked bar plots comparing the conversion rate to paid customers for the different categories. To do this a new stacked barplot function is first made, based on the one of the previous assessment.

In [36]:
def stacked_bar_plot(df, to_predict, influencing_factor):
  """
  Creates a stacked barplot to visualize the distribution of a variable with limited discrete options across categories in a Pandas DataFrame.

  Args:
      df (pd.DataFrame): The Pandas DataFrame containing the data.
      to_predict (str): The name of the categorical variable to group the data by.
      influencing_factor (str): The name of the categorical variable with variable with limited discrete options.

  Returns:
      None. This function creates a stacked barplot and displays it using plt.show().
  """
  # Group by category and count occurrences of each score
  grouped = df.groupby([influencing_factor, to_predict]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

  # Calculate the row-wise sum
  row_sums = grouped.sum(axis=1)

  # Divide each element by the row sum and multiply by 100 to get the percentage
  df_normalized = grouped.div(row_sums, axis=0)*100

  # Plot it in a stacked bar plot
  df_normalized.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True)
  plt.gca().set_ylabel("%")

  # Add the ylabel
  plt.xlabel(variable_name(influencing_factor))

  # Add title
  plt.title(f'Distribution of the {variable_name(influencing_factor)} for {variable_name(to_predict)}')

  plt.show()

  return
Current Occupation¶
In [37]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'status', 'current_occupation')

Observations:

  • We see a big difference in the conversion to paid customers for especially students. Because there are not so financially able to pay for the courses, only a low percentage of them takes the program.
  • At the other hand, we see the percentage of professionals taken the program to be higher. This is probably because they can afford it or that they need/want it for further steps in their carreer.

This answers one of the questions posed in the beginning. So yes, the occupation has a big impact on the fact that it could be a paying customer or not.

It could be interesting to look once at the age distribution for each of the different occupations. For clarity, we will combine ages in age groups (<30, 30-40, 40-50, 50+). This will be done using a new function:

In [38]:
def categorize_age(df):
  """
  Categorizes ages in a DataFrame into age groups (<30, 30-40, 40-50, 50+).

  Args:
    df: The pandas DataFrame containing the 'age' column.

  Returns:
    The DataFrame with a new 'age_group' column containing the categorized ages.
  """
  df_age = df.copy()
  bins = [0, 30, 40, 50, float('inf')]  # Define age bins
  labels = ['<30', '30-40', '40-50', '50+']  # Define labels for each bin

  df_age['age_group'] = pd.cut(df_age['age'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)
  return df_age

The dataframe with the age groups is then:

In [39]:
df_with_age_groups = categorize_age(df)

The same bar plot function helps us now to visualize the age distribution for the different occupation categories:

In [40]:
stacked_bar_plot(df_with_age_groups, 'age_group', 'current_occupation')

We see a big correlation between the current occupation on the age. This is kind of predictable, given the fact that most students are younger than 30 and professionals are typically older. As students are less likely to become a paying customer, it might make sense to only use one of the two columns (age or occupation), as they are both containing very similar data. It might be an interesting excercise to check this once. In the scope of this excercise we will however keep all the data, to be as accurate as possible.

First interaction¶
In [41]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'status', 'first_interaction')

The conversion for users using the website first is a lot higher than when they use the mobile app.

This answers one of the questions posed in the beginning. So yes, the type of first interaction has a big impact on the fact that it could be a paying customer or not.

Profile Completed¶
In [42]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'status', 'profile_completed')

Observations:

  • As expected, the chance of conversion rate for people who filled in their profile the most is the highest. They show most interest.
  • For the lowest fill rate the interest is also lowest.

This answers one of the questions posed in the beginning. So yes, a profile that is filled better means as well that the person is more likely to become a paying customer.

Last Activity¶
In [43]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'status', 'last_activity')

Observations:

  • The conversion rate for website as last activity is the highest
  • The conversion rate for phone as last activity is the lowest
Others¶

In the bivariate analysis of the numerical features, we used dummy variables which showed us that the impact of e.g. 'print_media_type1' was not present on the lead conversion status. A stacked barplot can confirm this once again for the categorical version of this feature:

In [44]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'status', 'print_media_type1')

As expected, we do not see any big influence on the conversion rate based on the 'print_media_type1'. This confirms what was seen in the heatmap earlier. The same is true for the 'print_media_type2', 'digital_media' and 'educational_channels'.

The same heatmap showed however an influence of a referral. The stacked barplot confirms this again:

In [45]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'status', 'referral')

We can expect a big impact of a referral on the final conversion rate. Keep however in mind that a referral was only present in 2% of the total cases.

This immediately answers the last remaining question posed above: the channels that has the biggest impact on the lead conversion rate is by far the 'referral' one.

Note that we could also check the influence between different types of advertissements (like here below between digital media and educational channels). No clear correlations are however seen when that is done for all these types. One example (between 'educational channels' and 'digital media) is given below:

In [46]:
stacked_bar_plot(df, 'educational_channels', 'digital_media')

Building a Decision Tree model¶

We want to model a prediction for the which leads are likely to be converted to paying customers. In order to build a model, we need to perform the following steps:

  1. Analyze the data, clean if needed, impute missing data and eliminate potential outliers. - This has been done in the previous part.
  2. Perform a bi-variate analysis to see which parameters impact which other ones. Especially with a focus on the 'status' here, as this is the target of the prediction. - This has been done in the previous part.
  3. When the model needs to be build, we need to further prepare the data by * removing unnecessary columns which will not have any impact on the conversion data predicted by the model (e.g. ID numbers) * split the data in the dependent (target) and independent variables * making dummy variables for the independent categorical variables * splitting the data in test and train data sets.
  4. Make sure we have a measure to rate the performance of the built models.

The steps in point 3 and 4 will now be addressed.

3 - Preparation¶

a) Removing the ID column. This was already done earlier.

b) Splitting the data in the dependent (target y) and independent variables (X):

In [47]:
y = df['status']
X = df.drop(["status"], axis=1)

We print the independent variables once to see if everything looks correct:

In [48]:
X.head()
Out[48]:
age current_occupation first_interaction profile_completed website_visits time_spent_on_website page_views_per_visit last_activity print_media_type1 print_media_type2 digital_media educational_channels referral
0 57 Unemployed Website High 7 1639 1.861 Website Activity Yes No Yes No No
1 56 Professional Mobile App Medium 2 83 0.320 Website Activity No No No Yes No
2 52 Professional Website Medium 3 330 0.074 Website Activity No No Yes No No
3 53 Unemployed Website High 4 464 2.057 Website Activity No No No No No
4 23 Student Website High 4 600 16.914 Email Activity No No No No No

c) We create the dummy columns for the categorical independent variables:

In [49]:
# list of the dummy columns
dummy_columns=['current_occupation', 'first_interaction', 'profile_completed', 'last_activity', 'print_media_type1',	'print_media_type2',	'digital_media',	'educational_channels',	'referral']

We make the dummy columns and drop the initial categorical ones:

In [50]:
X=pd.get_dummies(X, columns=dummy_columns, drop_first=True)

Another check of 'X' to see if all is ok:

In [51]:
X.head()
Out[51]:
age website_visits time_spent_on_website page_views_per_visit current_occupation_Student current_occupation_Unemployed first_interaction_Website profile_completed_Low profile_completed_Medium last_activity_Phone Activity last_activity_Website Activity print_media_type1_Yes print_media_type2_Yes digital_media_Yes educational_channels_Yes referral_Yes
0 57 7 1639 1.861 False True True False False False True True False True False False
1 56 2 83 0.320 False False False False True False True False False False True False
2 52 3 330 0.074 False False True False True False True False False True False False
3 53 4 464 2.057 False True True False False False True False False False False False
4 23 4 600 16.914 True False True False False False False False False False False False

We print the info on this dataframe as well, to see if that is ok:

In [52]:
X.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 4612 entries, 0 to 4611
Data columns (total 16 columns):
 #   Column                          Non-Null Count  Dtype  
---  ------                          --------------  -----  
 0   age                             4612 non-null   int64  
 1   website_visits                  4612 non-null   int64  
 2   time_spent_on_website           4612 non-null   int64  
 3   page_views_per_visit            4612 non-null   float64
 4   current_occupation_Student      4612 non-null   bool   
 5   current_occupation_Unemployed   4612 non-null   bool   
 6   first_interaction_Website       4612 non-null   bool   
 7   profile_completed_Low           4612 non-null   bool   
 8   profile_completed_Medium        4612 non-null   bool   
 9   last_activity_Phone Activity    4612 non-null   bool   
 10  last_activity_Website Activity  4612 non-null   bool   
 11  print_media_type1_Yes           4612 non-null   bool   
 12  print_media_type2_Yes           4612 non-null   bool   
 13  digital_media_Yes               4612 non-null   bool   
 14  educational_channels_Yes        4612 non-null   bool   
 15  referral_Yes                    4612 non-null   bool   
dtypes: bool(12), float64(1), int64(3)
memory usage: 198.3 KB

We have 16 columns remaining. No categorical ones, but just dummy boolean columns and also our temporary numerical columns are not present anymore (e.g. 'print_media_type1_num' from earlier).

d) We split the data in test and train data sets (70-30 ratio). We use the stratify setting to make sure the frequencies between lead conversions are similar between the test and train datasets. I will use random state 1 for repeatability (could be 42 as well).

In [53]:
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X,y,test_size=0.30,random_state=1,stratify=y)

We should still check if it all makes sense now:

In [54]:
print("The dataframe shapes are as follows:")
print(f"X_train: {X_train.shape}")
print(f"X_test: {X_test.shape}")
print(f"y_train: {y_train.shape}")
print(f"y_test: {y_test.shape}")

The dataframe shapes are as follows:
X_train: (3228, 16)
X_test: (1384, 16)
y_train: (3228,)
y_test: (1384,)

30% is test dataset and 70% train. Let's also check if the 'stratify function did its work:

In [55]:
print(y_train.sum()/y_train.count())
print(y_test.sum()/y_test.count())

0.298636926889715
0.2984104046242775

The ratio of lead conversions is for both the train and test dataset around 0.298. That is what we wanted.

4 - Model evaluation¶

As usual, we can build many different models. But we need to ask ourselves the question: what is the most important factor for this model?

A model can predict false positives:

The model predicts that a lead will convert, but eventually it will not. When this happens, we will lose resources on a lead that would not convert anyway. This is not desired.

A model can predict false negatives:

The model predicts that a lead will not convert, but it would have done it if treated in the correct way. When this happens, we will lose income and a new paying customer. This is also not desired.

Logically, the loss of a potential income (false negatives) is worse than wasting temporarily some resources. Therefor, we will try to reduce the amount of false negatives in our models. This means that we should aim at higher recall value.

A function that contains the classification report, shows the confusion matrix (all part of sklearn.metrics) will help to better judge the performance of the models that we build:

In [56]:
# Function that decribes the model metrics scores
def metrics_score(actual, predicted):
  """
  Calculates and displays classification model performance metrics.

  Args:
      actual (array-like): Ground truth labels for the classification task.
      predicted (array-like): Predicted labels by the classification model.
  """

  # Classification report
  print("Classification Report:\n")
  print(classification_report(actual, predicted))

  # Confusion matrix
  cm = confusion_matrix(actual, predicted)
  plt.figure(figsize=(7,6))

  # Heatmap with annotations
  sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True,  fmt='.0f')
  plt.title('Confusion Matrix')
  plt.ylabel('Actual')
  plt.xlabel('Predicted')

  plt.show()

Building the Decision Tree¶

Now, building the decision tree goes pretty fast with the available libraries:

In [57]:
# We use the decision tree classifier to fit on the training data set. We add the class weights (which we just calculated), as it is unbalanced.
model_dt = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight = {0: 0.298, 1: 0.702})
model_dt.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[57]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, random_state=1)

But of course the performances needs to be evaluated:

In [58]:
# Evaluate performance on the training dataset
pred_train_dt = model_dt.predict(X_train)
metrics_score(y_train, pred_train_dt)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       1.00      1.00      1.00      2264
           1       1.00      1.00      1.00       964

    accuracy                           1.00      3228
   macro avg       1.00      1.00      1.00      3228
weighted avg       1.00      1.00      1.00      3228

The recall and precision are both 1 in this model, which is of course very suspicious and will probably mean that we are overfitting and the we need to prune the tree. We can confirm this when we compare with the test dataset performance:

In [59]:
# Evaluate performance on the test dataset
pred_test_dt = model_dt.predict(X_test)
metrics_score(y_test, pred_test_dt)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.84      0.86      0.85       971
           1       0.66      0.63      0.64       413

    accuracy                           0.79      1384
   macro avg       0.75      0.75      0.75      1384
weighted avg       0.79      0.79      0.79      1384

The model scores a lot worse on the test dataset, compared to the training dataset. This indicates overfitting. We will need to take action to improve the model's performance.

Do we need to prune the tree?¶

As written at the end of the previous section, the performance of our model on the newly seen test dataset is a lot worse than on the training dataset. As happens often with decision trees, this means we are overfitting the model. We will need to tune the tree to reduce overfitting and better balance it for new unseen datasets.

We can play with different parameters e.g.

  • maximum depth
  • criteria
  • minimum samples split
  • minimum samples leaf
  • maximum features
  • minimum weight fraction leaf

to tune the tree.

The search for the best combination can be done with a grid search, a random search, a Bayesian optimization,...

We will use GridSearch.

Grid search¶

In [60]:
# Choose the type of classifier, we add the class weights, as it is unbalanced. Without these class weights, the results are worse for what we want to model for.
model_dt_tuned = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state = 1, class_weight = {0: 0.298, 1: 0.702})

# Grid of parameters to choose from
parameters = {'max_depth': np.arange(2, 8),
              'criterion': ['gini', 'entropy'],
              #'min_samples_leaf': [5, 10, 20, 25], # Did not have a big impact, omitted for further calculations and trials to speed up
              #'min_samples_split': [5, 10, 20, 25], # Did not have a big impact, omitted for further calculations and trials to speed up
              'max_features': [0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 'auto', 'sqrt','log2']
             }

As the recall score for converted leads (y=1) needs to be as high as possible, we can specify this more clearly with make scoring when we run the grid search. We could also build another scorer ourselves, but that does not look necessary here with the 'recall' feature.

In [61]:
# Run the search
grid_obj = GridSearchCV(model_dt_tuned, parameters, cv=5,scoring='recall',n_jobs=-1)
grid_obj = grid_obj.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Set the classifier to the best combination of parameters
model_dt_tuned = grid_obj.best_estimator_

# Fit the best algorithm to the data.
model_dt_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[61]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, max_depth=4,
                       max_features='sqrt', random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, max_depth=4,
                       max_features='sqrt', random_state=1)
In [62]:
# Checking performance on the training dataset
dt_pred_tuned_train = model_dt_tuned.predict(X_train)
metrics_score(y_train,dt_pred_tuned_train)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.97      0.63      0.77      2264
           1       0.53      0.96      0.68       964

    accuracy                           0.73      3228
   macro avg       0.75      0.80      0.72      3228
weighted avg       0.84      0.73      0.74      3228
In [63]:
# Checking performance on the test dataset
dt_pred_tuned_test = model_dt_tuned.predict(X_test)
metrics_score(y_test,dt_pred_tuned_test)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.97      0.61      0.75       971
           1       0.51      0.96      0.67       413

    accuracy                           0.72      1384
   macro avg       0.74      0.79      0.71      1384
weighted avg       0.84      0.72      0.73      1384

Conclusions:

  • Many different searches and combinations (which I did not keep here in the code), showed that the tuning parameters 'max_depth', 'criterion' and 'max_features' had the biggest impact on the result. A parameter like 'min_samples_leaf' did not change so much to the outcome, neither did changing between 'entropy' or 'gini' as criterion.

  • We see that for a maximum depth of of 4 and using the 'sqrt' (only the square root of the total number of features for each split) we achieve a model with a recall of 96% for the target of y=1. This is a high number, exactly what we aimed for.

  • The precision score of only about 0.53 for y=1 is not that good, so often resources will be spilled in trying to convince a dead-end lead. However, we decided initially that this is a lot less worse than losing the potential income of a lead that would have converted. Precision and recall are typically a trade-off. If we aim for a better recall, it will come with a cost for the precision.

  • The results for the test and train set are very similar, this indicates that we do not really have overfitting anymore.

Tree visualization and feature importance¶

In order to understand the model a bit more, it can be visualized and we can check which of the different features has in fact the most impact.

In [64]:
# Plot the tree till maximum depth of 4
feature_names = list(X_train.columns)
plt.figure(figsize=(30, 10))
tree.plot_tree(model_dt_tuned, max_depth=4, feature_names=feature_names, filled=True, fontsize=8, node_ids=False, class_names=None)

plt.show()

Blue in the plot represents conversion of a lead to a paying customer. Orange indicates leads that will most likely not convert.

What the plot learns us is that the time spent on the website is considered the first deciding factor. Then they look at the profile completion and the occupation. In the bivariate analysis we saw exactly those parameters are having an impact on the final conversion rate. As referral was only present in 2% of all cases, it is understandable it is not prevailing in the tree.

When looking at the feature importance, this is largely confirmed again. The following is the list of the different features in our model and their relative importance:

In [65]:
print(round(pd.DataFrame(model_dt_tuned.feature_importances_, columns = ["importance [%]"], index = X_train.columns).sort_values(by = 'importance [%]', ascending = False)*100,2))

                                importance [%]
time_spent_on_website                    37.46
first_interaction_Website                27.79
profile_completed_Medium                 20.97
current_occupation_Student                7.90
last_activity_Website Activity            2.90
current_occupation_Unemployed             1.52
last_activity_Phone Activity              1.24
age                                       0.16
page_views_per_visit                      0.05
educational_channels_Yes                  0.02
website_visits                            0.00
profile_completed_Low                     0.00
print_media_type1_Yes                     0.00
print_media_type2_Yes                     0.00
digital_media_Yes                         0.00
referral_Yes                              0.00
In [66]:
# Feature importances
importances = model_dt_tuned.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices],align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()

We see that the time spent on the website is the most important feature (37.4%), followed by the first interaction on the website (27.8%) and the completeness of the profile (20.9%). Note that the completeness of the profile basically differentiates between a high or medium filled profile, as the profiles are only first 'low' in 2.5% of the cases.

We can see that the current occupation is important, but the age is not so. However, we know there is a link between them, as we discussed before. So an alternative model might have swapped the influences of these two (see also the second decision tree model we build in the following).

Grid search 2¶

We could repeat the grid search, but e.g. tune less parameters.

In [67]:
# Choose the type of classifier, we add the class weights, as it is unbalanced. Without these class weights, the results are worse for what we want to model for.
model_dt_tuned_2 = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state = 1, class_weight = {0: 0.298, 1: 0.702})

# Grid of parameters to choose from
parameters = {'max_depth': np.arange(2, 8),
              'criterion': ['entropy', 'gini'],
             }

Here we do not consider the maximum features as a parameter to tune.

In [68]:
# Run the search
grid_obj = GridSearchCV(model_dt_tuned_2, parameters, cv=5,scoring='recall',n_jobs=-1)
grid_obj = grid_obj.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Set the classifier to the best combination of parameters
model_dt_tuned_2 = grid_obj.best_estimator_

# Fit the best algorithm to the data.
model_dt_tuned_2.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[68]:
DecisionTreeClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, criterion='entropy',
                       max_depth=3, random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
DecisionTreeClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, criterion='entropy',
                       max_depth=3, random_state=1)

We check the performance:

In [69]:
# Checking performance on the training dataset
dt_pred_tuned_train_2 = model_dt_tuned_2.predict(X_train)
metrics_score(y_train,dt_pred_tuned_train_2)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.95      0.75      0.84      2264
           1       0.60      0.91      0.72       964

    accuracy                           0.79      3228
   macro avg       0.78      0.83      0.78      3228
weighted avg       0.85      0.79      0.80      3228
In [70]:
# Checking performance on the test dataset
dt_pred_tuned_test_2 = model_dt_tuned_2.predict(X_test)
metrics_score(y_test,dt_pred_tuned_test_2)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.94      0.73      0.82       971
           1       0.58      0.89      0.70       413

    accuracy                           0.78      1384
   macro avg       0.76      0.81      0.76      1384
weighted avg       0.83      0.78      0.79      1384

We can see that the model is still well tuned, but slightly worse than the tuning including the 'maximum features' parameter done earlier. In this case, the model is however also slightly simpler, with only a maximum depth of 3.

The recall of this model is a bit lower (around 0.9) and the precision a bit higher (around 0.6). As always, no model is perfect and it will be a trade-off. It is just good to see that with a fairly simple model we can still reach satisfactory results here.

Let's visualize the tree again:

In [71]:
# Plot the second  tuned tree till maximum depth of 3
feature_names = list(X_train.columns)
plt.figure(figsize=(30, 10))
tree.plot_tree(model_dt_tuned_2, max_depth=3, feature_names=feature_names, filled=True, fontsize=8, node_ids=False, class_names=None)

plt.show()

The color code in the tree is the same as in the previous model.

The feature importances for this second model are as follows:

In [72]:
print(round(pd.DataFrame(model_dt_tuned_2.feature_importances_, columns = ["importance [%]"], index = X_train.columns).sort_values(by = 'importance [%]', ascending = False)*100,2))

                                importance [%]
time_spent_on_website                    36.56
first_interaction_Website                31.34
profile_completed_Medium                 23.38
last_activity_Phone Activity              4.82
last_activity_Website Activity            3.20
age                                       0.69
website_visits                            0.00
page_views_per_visit                      0.00
current_occupation_Student                0.00
current_occupation_Unemployed             0.00
profile_completed_Low                     0.00
print_media_type1_Yes                     0.00
print_media_type2_Yes                     0.00
digital_media_Yes                         0.00
educational_channels_Yes                  0.00
referral_Yes                              0.00
In [73]:
# Feature importances
importances = model_dt_tuned_2.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices],align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()

Here the main feature are largely similar as in the first model (first 3 are the same), but the age is basically replacing the occupation feature in the list of important parameters (however less important). The last activity is also more important. All the other features have no impact.

The small differences between the similar decision tree models 1 and 2 show that no model is perfect and variations are possible. Maybe due to the greater simplicity the second one might be preferred, but the recall score (=our target) of the first one is slightly better.

Building a Random Forest model¶

Similar to the decision tree, we start building the untuned model

In [74]:
# We use the decision tree classifier to fit on the training data set. We add the class weights (which we just calculated), as it is unbalanced.
model_rf = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight = {0: 0.298, 1: 0.702})
model_rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[74]:
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, random_state=1)

We check the performance of the first model:

In [75]:
# Evaluate performance on the training dataset
pred_train_rf = model_rf.predict(X_train)
metrics_score(y_train, pred_train_rf)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       1.00      1.00      1.00      2264
           1       1.00      1.00      1.00       964

    accuracy                           1.00      3228
   macro avg       1.00      1.00      1.00      3228
weighted avg       1.00      1.00      1.00      3228

The suspiciously good recall and precision points towards overfitting.

In [76]:
# Evaluate performance on the training dataset
pred_test_rf = model_rf.predict(X_test)
metrics_score(y_test, pred_test_rf)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.88      0.92      0.90       971
           1       0.80      0.71      0.75       413

    accuracy                           0.86      1384
   macro avg       0.84      0.82      0.83      1384
weighted avg       0.86      0.86      0.86      1384

Even tough the performance on the test set is not too bad, it is a lot worse than the training set (and the previous decision tree models). This indicates that there is probably overfitting again. So we should could reduce the overfitting and improve the recall by hyperparameter tuning again.

Do we need to prune the tree?¶

As just mentioned, the differences in performances between the test and train dataset, indicate that we should tune the model. In addition to the hyperparameters of the decision tree, there are some more possible here, like 'n_estimators'. We could take some of the parameters already closer to the ones obtained from the decision tree, but for clarity we will run a nearly complete analysis again. This will of course take a while to complete...

After that we can fine-tune a bit more and re-run.

In [77]:
# Choose the type of classifier, we add the class weights, as it is unbalanced. Without these class weights, the results are worse for what we want to model for.
model_rf_tuned = RandomForestClassifier(random_state = 1, class_weight = {0: 0.298, 1: 0.702})

# Grid of parameters to choose from
parameters = {'max_depth': np.arange(2, 8),
              'criterion': ['entropy'], # First run showed 'entropy' to be preferred, 'gini' taken out after that
              'max_features': [0.7, 0.8, 0.9], # First run showed '0.8' to be preferred, only 0.7, 0.8 and 0.9 kept in consecutive re-runs ('srqt', 0.6, 0.5 not considered anymore)
              'n_estimators': [50, 75], # First run preferred 50 above 100, second run was with 75 added
              "min_samples_leaf": [10, 20], # First run preferred 20 above 25, second run was with 10 added
              "max_samples": [0.7, 0.8, 0.9], # First run preferred 0.8, later runs where focusing around this value
             }
In [78]:
# Run the search
grid_obj = GridSearchCV(model_rf_tuned, parameters, cv=5,scoring='recall',n_jobs=-1)
grid_obj = grid_obj.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Set the classifier to the best combination of parameters
model_rf_tuned = grid_obj.best_estimator_

# Fit the best algorithm to the data.
model_rf_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[78]:
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, criterion='entropy',
                       max_depth=3, max_features=0.7, max_samples=0.9,
                       min_samples_leaf=10, n_estimators=75, random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight={0: 0.298, 1: 0.702}, criterion='entropy',
                       max_depth=3, max_features=0.7, max_samples=0.9,
                       min_samples_leaf=10, n_estimators=75, random_state=1)
In [79]:
# Checking performance on the training dataset
rf_pred_tuned_train = model_rf_tuned.predict(X_train)
metrics_score(y_train,rf_pred_tuned_train)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.95      0.75      0.84      2264
           1       0.61      0.91      0.73       964

    accuracy                           0.80      3228
   macro avg       0.78      0.83      0.78      3228
weighted avg       0.85      0.80      0.81      3228
In [80]:
# Checking performance on the test dataset
rf_pred_tuned_test = model_rf_tuned.predict(X_test)
metrics_score(y_test,rf_pred_tuned_test)

Classification Report:

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.94      0.74      0.83       971
           1       0.59      0.89      0.71       413

    accuracy                           0.78      1384
   macro avg       0.76      0.81      0.77      1384
weighted avg       0.84      0.78      0.79      1384

Conclusions:

  • Some consecutive re-runs were done, playing with different hyperparameters and improving the model. However, at the end none of them significantly improved the performance on the train/test set.
  • The achieved level (with a recall of around 90%) is however already satisfying. It is remarkable to see that the random forest is performing at a similar level as the simpler decision tree model, but is slightly worse (96% recall for our first decision tree model).
  • Again the accuracy level is a pay-off with the recall.

Visualizing a random forest is hard and it is not possible to analyse it is a similar way as a decision tree. We can however look at the feature importance again.

In [81]:
print(round(pd.DataFrame(model_rf_tuned.feature_importances_, columns = ["importance [%]"], index = X_train.columns).sort_values(by = 'importance [%]', ascending = False)*100,4))

                                importance [%]
time_spent_on_website                  36.2614
first_interaction_Website              36.1408
profile_completed_Medium               17.3946
last_activity_Phone Activity            3.2872
last_activity_Website Activity          2.6862
age                                     2.0364
current_occupation_Student              1.5197
profile_completed_Low                   0.3421
current_occupation_Unemployed           0.1102
page_views_per_visit                    0.0986
website_visits                          0.0880
referral_Yes                            0.0348
print_media_type2_Yes                   0.0000
print_media_type1_Yes                   0.0000
digital_media_Yes                       0.0000
educational_channels_Yes                0.0000
In [82]:
# Feature importances
importances = model_rf_tuned.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices],align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()

Conclusions:

  • As similar order of importances can be found as for the decision tree models. 'time spent on website', 'first_interaction_website' and 'profile_completed_medium' are the 3 most important features.
  • In contrast with the decision tree, there are more features with a non-zero influence. In fact, when listing the importances without rounding we can see that only 'digital_media' and 'educational_channels' have no impact in the model.
In [83]:

                                importance [%]

digital_media_Yes                 0.000000e+00
educational_channels_Yes          0.000000e+00

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

Model performances:¶

We tuned the decision tree twice and the random forest once. In general, the results are very similar, with these results:

In [84]:
print("First tuned decision tree:")
print(classification_report(y_test,dt_pred_tuned_test))
print("-"*60)
print("Second tuned decision tree:")
print(classification_report(y_test,dt_pred_tuned_test_2))
print("-"*60)
print("Tuned random forest:")
print(classification_report(y_test,rf_pred_tuned_test))
print("-"*60)

First tuned decision tree:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.97      0.61      0.75       971
           1       0.51      0.96      0.67       413

    accuracy                           0.72      1384
   macro avg       0.74      0.79      0.71      1384
weighted avg       0.84      0.72      0.73      1384

------------------------------------------------------------
Second tuned decision tree:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.94      0.73      0.82       971
           1       0.58      0.89      0.70       413

    accuracy                           0.78      1384
   macro avg       0.76      0.81      0.76      1384
weighted avg       0.83      0.78      0.79      1384

------------------------------------------------------------
Tuned random forest:
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.94      0.74      0.83       971
           1       0.59      0.89      0.71       413

    accuracy                           0.78      1384
   macro avg       0.76      0.81      0.77      1384
weighted avg       0.84      0.78      0.79      1384

------------------------------------------------------------

Observations:

  • The recall for the conversions (y=1) is best for the first tuned decision tree (96%), but the difference with the second decision tree and the random forest is not too big. Given the simplicity of the second decision tree (less depth), this model might be preferred in some cases.

  • The recall is a trade-off with the precision, which is for y=1 typically around 0.6 (second decision tree and random forest) or 0.5 (first decision tree)

Feature importances:¶
In [85]:
print("Importance features")
print("-"*60)
print("First tuned decision tree:")
print(pd.DataFrame(model_dt_tuned.feature_importances_, columns = ["importance [%]"], index = X_train.columns).sort_values(by = 'importance [%]', ascending = False)[0:5]*100)
print("-"*60)
print("Second tuned decision tree:")
print(pd.DataFrame(model_dt_tuned_2.feature_importances_, columns = ["importance [%]"], index = X_train.columns).sort_values(by = 'importance [%]', ascending = False)[0:5]*100)
print("-"*60)
print("Tuned random forest:")
print(pd.DataFrame(model_rf_tuned.feature_importances_, columns = ["importance [%]"], index = X_train.columns).sort_values(by = 'importance [%]', ascending = False)[0:5]*100)

Importance features
------------------------------------------------------------
First tuned decision tree:
                                importance [%]
time_spent_on_website                37.457308
first_interaction_Website            27.790547
profile_completed_Medium             20.966677
current_occupation_Student            7.898949
last_activity_Website Activity        2.900032
------------------------------------------------------------
Second tuned decision tree:
                                importance [%]
time_spent_on_website                36.564351
first_interaction_Website            31.341039
profile_completed_Medium             23.382563
last_activity_Phone Activity          4.823640
last_activity_Website Activity        3.200302
------------------------------------------------------------
Tuned random forest:
                                importance [%]
time_spent_on_website                36.261353
first_interaction_Website            36.140800
profile_completed_Medium             17.394578
last_activity_Phone Activity          3.287196
last_activity_Website Activity        2.686182

Observations:

  • For sure the 'time spent on the website', the 'first interaction' and the 'profile completion' are important parameters. They are the prevailing ones in all three models. Logically, their impact is not the same for all three models.
  • the 'last_activity' is the 4th/5th most important feature in the 2nd and 3rd model. The first model however takes 'occupation' more into account.
Recommandations:¶
  • Our first tuned decision tree gives the best recall value. As that is preferred, this is the best model to use.
  • Focus should be on the main influencing parameters like 'time spent on the website', the 'first interaction' and the 'profile completion'. When the last activity is on the website, the conversion chance in higher, the same is valid if the profile is filled to a higher degree.
  • Because the conversion rate is a lot higher when the first interaction is over the website, one could question if the mobile application should be improved. Is there a reason why the conversion rate would be so much lower there?
  • The age or occupation has also influence on the conversion rate. Younger people/students buy the educational package a lot less. A dedicated student package (cheap deal) to increase the conversion for students could therefor be an interesting path to follow.
